data.title: Forget Buy-And-Hold: The High-Yield Strategy That Supercharged My Results
data.description:
Many investors like to subscribe to the mantra of buy and hold forever. In fact, this idea is supported by Warren Buffett and Charlie Munger, who took the viewpoint that the ideal holding period for them was forever. While this sounds good in theory, and there are many pros to buying and holding wonderful businesses for the long term, when it comes to most high-yielding stocks, I am firmly in the camp that buy and hold is more often than not a suboptimal strategy. In this article, I will detail why.

The Buy-and-hold Argument: Why It Sounds So Convincing

The main argument in favor of buying and holding forever boils down to a couple of key pillars. First and foremost, there's the tax benefit. If you buy and hold forever, you never have to realize capital gains, which means you don't have to pay capital gains taxes, and then you can pass the investment onto your heirs, who get a step-up in cost basis, making those gains effectively tax-free. Or alternatively, you can donate those shares to a charity that you are passionate about supporting and can get a full tax write-off on the value of those shares without ever having to pay taxes on the gains that you made, making it an extremely tax-efficient way to give to a cause you care about. Moreover, during that period, the retained earnings can compound tax-free, leading to outsized long-term tax-adjusted total returns.

The other big argument in favor of buying and holding forever, even in a Roth IRA, for example, where taxes are not a consideration, is that you remove emotions from the equation and simply let the power of compounding do its work. There is an abundance of data to support this, including the fact that portfolios that retail investors forget about actually tend to outperform over the long term compared to those that are closely watched and actively managed by retail investors. This makes sense, too, when simply thinking about it, because when obsessing over your stock returns each day during market sell-offs, it is easy to give in to the panic and the scary headlines and sell near the bottom. Then, when everything is great in the market and stocks are soaring higher and higher day after day, you may become tempted to chase hot stocks higher and end up grossly overpaying for them.

This idea of trading in and out then can lead you into a very deadly spiral of selling when stocks are on sale and buying when stocks are expensive, thereby doing the opposite of what a good value investor does. Not only that, but even if you're trying to be guided by value investing principles and buying low and selling high, you can be tricked into thinking that just because a stock has been doing well recently or it looks expensive today in terms of a traditional valuation multiple, it is not a good buy and that you should instead sell it to lock in profits in hopes of buying back in down the road. However, the reality may turn out to be that it is simply a wonderful company that has incredible amounts of growth in store for it ahead, and therefore, you end up selling a company like Nvidia (NVDA), Tesla (TSLA), Apple (AAPL), or Amazon (AMZN) way too early and giving up incredible gains down the road.

These are all very valid points, and I do not want to disparage them in any way. In fact, for someone who invests in high-quality, growth-dominated compounding machines, I think in many cases this is a good way to approach it. Additionally, if I were primarily an index (SPY)(QQQ) investor or a growth investor, I would invest with a buy-and-hold mindset unless I had a very compelling reason to sell beyond something like valuation concerns.

Why High-yield Stocks Are a Different Animal

However, as someone who invests in the high-yield space, I think it is important to view this idea of buying and selling stocks quite differently. The reasons for this are quite simple. First of all, a high-yield stock is almost always high-yield for a reason. It is either because it is highly risky or because it has very low growth expectations, and sometimes both.

As a qualitative and value-oriented investor, I try very hard to avoid the high-risk, high-yield stocks and instead look for businesses that have sustainable high yields but also deliver steady growth that is likely to meet or beat inflation over time, thereby preserving the purchasing power of the yield that it pays out. In this case, it is very unlikely that it is going to dramatically surprise to the upside in terms of its growth. Therefore, the concern that I simply misunderstand the company and I'm selling it way too soon before it has this incredible growth spurt ahead of it is highly unlikely. Meanwhile, as long as I'm focused on strict valuation and qualitative data with these companies, I am much less likely to fall prey to my emotions of selling when the market is crashing and buying out of fear when it's soaring, and instead intelligently buy low and sell high.

However, this still does not address the tax component of the argument in favor of taking a buy-and-hold approach. I have two rebuttals to this. First of all, I tend to hold all but my UBTI-generated holdings in an IRA or 401k, thereby making the tax component irrelevant. Thus, by buying and selling stocks intelligently in aggregate, I actually can effectively accelerate the compounding process on my investments because I don't just get the yield and growth working for me, but then I also get valuation multiple expansion working for me because I wait until the valuation multiple expands to close to my fair value view, I sell the stock, recycle the capital into one that is trading at a deep discount on a valuation multiple basis, and then I wait patiently for that one to appreciate fairing value, and I rinse and repeat the process. Sure, not every one of my investments is going to be a winner, and sure, some I buy too soon and some I buy too late, but if I intelligently diversify across a couple of dozen of these companies and insist on quality and wide margins of safety in my buy prices, I end up significantly ahead over time.

Meanwhile, for the stocks that I hold in my taxable account, I am much more cautious about selling, as they are mostly K-1-issuing MLPs (AMLP) like Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Energy Transfer (ET), and others like them that I would ideally like to hold for long periods of time and earn tax-deferred income on. Moreover, I only sell when they become significantly overvalued, or I see another option to recycle the capital into something that is significantly undervalued relative to them that makes any tax I incur worthwhile to the switch.

Finally, keep in mind that the approach I am describing here is not one of short-term speculation and a trader mindset. On the contrary, I still maintain a long-term investment approach, with a keen focus on valuation and fundamentals, and whenever I buy a stock, it is with the expectation that I will end up holding it for a really long time. The only caveat here that separates it from a strict buy-and-hold strategy is that - if the market offers me an attractive price for a stock that I own - I am willing to take it up on its generosity and trim or sell the position in order to free up capital that I can recycle into another more undervalued opportunity that still conforms to my portfolio diversification targets. In so doing, I accelerate the compounding process in my portfolio, and I am unlikely to be selling many of my high-yield picks way too soon, given that by nature the sector tends to have very modest and predictable growth, making these sorts of stocks relatively moderate long-term wealth compounders outside a capital recycling strategy that can turn their 8-12% annualized return profiles into a 15-25% annualized return profile.

Investor Takeaway

While buy-and-hold investing is a fine approach, and I actually think it is the prudent approach for index investors and for growth investors, for a valuation- and fundamentals-focused high-yield investor, I think that an opportunistic capital recycling approach, especially when it is concentrated in an IRA and a 401k, is a much better approach. This is particularly true when executed in a tax-advantaged account and combined with strict valuation discipline.

This isn't just me saying this either. My results at High Yield Investor over the past five years in my portfolios have shown that my approach has enabled me to significantly outperform dividend stock funds, whether it be the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), the Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM), the Global X SuperDividend ETF (DIV), or even the S&P 500 (SPY) itself over that period of time with below-market beta.

data.title: Ingersoll Rand Q4 Review: Strong Pipeline, But Strategy Is Not Appealing For Now
data.description:
Introduction

Ingersoll Rand Inc. (IR) just reported its full-year results, so I wanted to take a look at the numbers in more detail and give some comments on the company and its outlook and why I think it is not a good time for me to invest in it for now.

By the Numbers

Sales for Q4 came in at around $2.09B, up a respectable 10.6% y/y, beating estimates by around $50m. Looking at the segment breakdown, the Industrial Technologies and Services segment, or IT&S, sales came in at $1.67B, up 11%, while the Precision and Science Technologies, or P&ST, segment sales came in at $419m, up 8%. Each respective segment’s organic growth was 3% and 4%, so most of the growth came from the company’s M&A strategy. In terms of order backlog, total orders came in at $7.7B for the year, which is up 9% y/y, which is a strong performance; however, most of the growth came from inorganic growth through M&As. For each segment, orders were up 9% (1% organic) for IT&S to $1.55B for the quarter. For P&ST, orders were up 7% (1% organic).

Looking at the company’s profitability, adjusted EBITDA for the year came in at around $2.094B, up 4%, with a margin of 27.4%. IT&S's adjusted EBITDA came in at $1.748B, with a 28.8% margin, while P&ST's adjusted EBITDA was $478m, with around a 30% margin. Compared to the previous year, the IT&S segment saw its profitability decline by around 130bps, while P&ST’s profitability improved by around 280bps, which helped offset some of the loss in profitability, but since the segment is much smaller than the other one, the overall adjusted EBITDA margin still compressed by around 50bps for the year. The management said this is largely due to macroeconomic headwinds and geopolitical factors. In other words, tariffs.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial position. IR finished the year with around $1.249B in cash and equivalents, against $4.78B in long-term debt. It makes sense that the company has quite a bit of debt on its books because the company is quite aggressive in its acquisition strategy, closing 16 deals for 2025. But is it a bit too aggressive? Looking at the company’s interest expense on the debt outstanding, we can safely say that no, it is not excessive. For the year, the company’s operating income was $1.144B, while its interest expense was around $254m, giving us an interest coverage ratio of around 4.5x. For reference, many analysts consider anything over 2x to be healthy.

Additionally, the company’s cash from operations was close to the company’s operating profits, at around $1.335B, and its free cash flow came in at a very healthy $1.22B. This gives us around a 91% conversion rate for the year. It’s a little under its 95% goal, but in the current macroenvironment full of tariffs and other uncertainties, it was a good result. The company remains a massive cash cow.

On top of these results, the management provided guidance for the full-year 2026. The management expects full-year 2026 sales growth to be 3.5% at the midpoint, which is around $7.92B for the year. Analysts expected sales to be around $7.96B. Adjusted EBITDA at the midpoint was given as $2.16B, while analysts expected $2.19B, and adjusted EPS is expected to be $3.51 at the midpoint, also below consensus estimates of $3.56. So, overall, it was a slight miss on all metrics provided.

On the day of the earnings, the company’s share price opened up around 5%, and then over the next 15 minutes gave back around 4%, and then over the rest of the day, it recovered once again and finished the day with almost 5% up, so it seems that the investors didn’t mind the slight miss, as it seems they valued the decent order performance a lot more as well as a decent beat on top and bottom lines. The margin expansion in P&ST, I think, played some decent role here too, as it expanded almost 3% y/y.

Overall, I would consider the Q4 report to be quite positive. The company’s strong balance sheet, decent operational performance despite tariff worries, and very healthy cash flow are helping the company’s bullish case.

Comments on the Outlook

As I mentioned earlier, the company’s growth strategy depends on its ability to continue to acquire smaller players in the same industry. In 2025, the company closed 16 deals, which helped to grow its top line as well as order backlog close to double digits. This strategy allows the company to be a massive compounder in the long term, and in short, it is the company’s primary growth strategy. The management already has its eyes on 9 additional acquisitions for 2026, which can only mean the company may easily add a lot more than the 16 companies it did in 2025. For the 16 companies it acquired, it paid a little over $525m, which added $275m in annualized inorganic revenue. I can easily see the company’s “bolt-on” strategy continuing strong for the next few years because the company’s cash flow easily allows that. As we saw earlier, the company’s cash flow from operations was over $1.3B, while its free cash flow was around $1.2B, which means the company has plenty of capital to go on a massive shopping spree to keep its strategy alive. Not to mention its mountain of cash on hand, as well as $2.6B of undrawn capacity under its credit facilities, giving $3.8B in liquidity.

That’s all great and all; however, I am a bit worried that this strategy cannot last forever. And even if it does, I don’t know how well the company can integrate all of these acquisitions to be sales and margin-accretive. For example, let’s take a look at the guidance the management provided: revenue growth for 2026 is around 3.5%, with M&As only contributing 1.5%, while organic growth is up to 2%. I know that the guidance doesn’t really include the future acquisitions, but it seems like a lot of work to improve its top-line performance so little. I’m sure it will be updated as quarters go by since the company will continue its bolt-on strategy of acquiring companies.

The management has been explicit about why they are doing this strategy. It is not about sales; it is more specifically about buying margins. So, as long as the company can become a lot more profitable, the strategy should be positive, but we saw that there was some compression over the last year, so it is not certain that future acquisitions will necessarily be margin accretive.

Should you buy Ingersoll?

I think if you are a fan of this strategy of growing through acquisitions, you would like to own Ingersoll; however, with a forward P/E ratio of almost 28x, the company seems to be priced to perfection, and any further slip-ups in the strategy could lead to a de-rating by many analysts. There are a lot of risks regarding this strategy, like integration overload. The IRX system could get stretched thin over time, which can lead to some hiccups in integration. Maybe even some cultural friction if the acquired companies are not used to the way things are at IR.

Being a compounder, the company doesn’t rely on a single massive innovation, think AI and data center demand currently, and doesn’t rely on a booming economy to grow. This is particularly helpful during times of a sideways economy. The company isn’t waiting for the economy to give it growth; it will continue to buy it and compound.

For me, though, the company seems a bit expensive for what it is. So, I think I will pass for now. There may be a time when I will be looking for such safe bets in a weird economy, but that is not the time yet.

data.title: PayPal: Despite Uncertainty, Stock Remains A Buy
data.description:
When I wrote my last article about PayPal (PYPL) in November, I was not expecting what had happened since then. Alex Chriss is no longer CEO; PayPal missed expectations for FY25 and set disappointing guidance. I believe that the narrative is partially broken now and uncertainty has increased. However, at a current market cap of below $40 billion, valuation is too cheap for a still-growing, highly profitable, and cash-generative business. I maintain my Buy Rating but reduce my price target from $100 to $70.

Fourth-quarter Results: Branded Checkout is an Issue Once Again

In the third quarter, PayPal delivered a double beat on revenue and EPS, and the guidance was raised for the full financial year. In the following months, the stock declined. Former CEO Alex Chriss mentioned at Citi’s 14th Annual FinTech conference that integration is taking longer than expected. Moreover, CFO Jamie Miller discussed at the UBS Global Technology and AI conference the ongoing macro pressure and headwinds for PayPal. Both conferences were warning signs that I should have taken more seriously.

The fourth quarter came as a real surprise. The company reported $8.68 billion in revenue for the quarter, which missed expectations by $90 million, and Non-GAAP EPS of $1.23, which is $0.06 below expectations. PayPal expected Non-GAAP EPS of $5.35-$5.39 for the full financial year and reported $5.31. It's not popular on Wall Street to raise guidance and then not to meet it. However, I don’t believe that these results were the reasons for the 20% sell-off. Missing on expectations by a few cents and revenue by $90 million on $8.68 billion shouldn’t cause a market value of $20 billion to evaporate.

Once again, the key concern was Branded Checkout, which has been the profit engine of PayPal for many years. At the beginning of the Internet, PayPal enabled secure payment and developed a trusted brand for consumers. That and frictionless payment and increased conversion made it more attractive for merchants to offer PayPal as a payment option. Until today, PayPal had been leading in the Branded Checkout in e-commerce. However, competition has heated up and taken market share. One of the reasons is that PayPal is no longer unique and lost its first-mover advantage. Today, shopping online is much more secure, and competitors offer very similar services. Fintech has evolved into a commodity, and merchants are less willing to pay higher take rates for PayPal’s products. Moreover, PayPal has underinvested for years and, therefore, created worse experiences for merchants and users.

Branded Checkout decelerated from 6% growth in Q4 2024 to just 1% in Q4 2025. In FY24, Branded Checkout grew at 6% and by 4% in FY25. In the conference call, Jamie Miller pointed out that this poor result was partially because of a softer macro environment. I tend to get cautious when management blames the macro environment for poor results. It's true that the American consumer is not doing that well; however, e-commerce companies such as Shopify have delivered revenue growth of 30%. The real picture is more complex than simply the environment PayPal is in.

Jamie Miller also named competition as a headwind, which I believe is the real reason for the results. Apple Pay, Google Pay, Sofi, Stripe, Adyen, Revolut, and many more are competing in the fintech space. Paying has become a commodity. As a consumer, I don’t care if I use the debit card of my regional bank or PayPal’s debit card.

Nevertheless, I don’t get why some investors claim that no one is using PayPal anymore, as more than 200 million people use it monthly. It's true that consumers use it less, as transactions per account decreased by 4%. However, a collapse of user activity can’t be observed. Moreover, the number of active accounts increased by 1%.

My own perception has changed over the last couple of months, and I see competition as a much bigger issue today. In my view, PayPal’s Branded Checkout could become a declining business if no proper action is taken.

The CFO also mentioned the difficulty of integration. Former CEO Alex Chriss and his team developed innovations such as Fastlane or updated the payment sheet to have BNPL upstream as a payment option. PayPal didn’t stand still over the last 2.5 years and improved the product. However, merchants required more hands-on integration support than anticipated, and it takes much longer to implement all those changes. You shouldn’t forget that PayPal is processing transactions worth $1.8 trillion annually and has millions of merchants. It takes time for the company to implement innovations and become more competitive.

To conclude, Branded Checkout seems to be at a critical stage right now, and I expected more in the last year. Jamie Miller put it the right way with “our execution is not yet where it needs to be.”

The Strategy is Working in Other Areas

There are three developments I see as very favorable for PayPal.

First, as I wrote in my previous article, the PSP segment artificially inflated growth numbers in the years but didn’t contribute that much to the bottom line. One key pillar of PayPal’s new strategy was to grow profitably in the PSP segment even if this meant slowing down growth for a couple of quarters. However, there was the concern that PayPal had “killed” its growth engine, which was proven wrong this quarter. PSP TPV growth accelerated to +8% FXN for the fourth quarter. Additionally, PSP contributed more to TM$ compared to just one year ago.

Moreover, Venmo grew its TPV by 12% in FY25, three percentage points more than in FY24. Venmo has 100 million users and generated $1.7 billion in revenues ahead of plans. ARPA grew by 14%. In my view, Venmo is a very valuable asset for the company. The app is used by teens and young adults, who are the consumers of tomorrow. Furthermore, it was barely monetized and primarily used for peer-to-peer transactions. The strategy shift two years ago made sense, and I think that it was executed well. It's the fifth consecutive quarter of double-digit growth. If Venmo continues to grow at double digits, it's likely to diversify the TPV mix more and offset some of the weaknesses of the Branded Checkout. Venmo currently makes 18% of total TPV, which increased by one percentage point compared to one year ago.

The third positive development is Omni growth. PayPal was primarily located in online payments and e-commerce. However, offline payments such as groceries offer an opportunity for PayPal to increase its TPV and strengthen its competitive advantage. I believe that consumers are looking for one app to do everything. PayPal has a bigger value proposition if it can be used online as well as offline. Moreover, the ARPA of US debit card users is three times larger than for checkout-only. I saw several people here in Germany use PayPal debit cards in the supermarket, which is a good sign that adoption is happening. This is also reflected in the numbers, with global debit TPV growing by 60% to $35 billion. However, it's important to stress that $35 billion is very low compared to $1.8 trillion total TPV. Nevertheless, it's a good sign that PayPal is expanding its presence and offering the user more opportunities to open the app and pay frictionlessly.

I understand that Branded Checkout remains the key focus of the market and analysts, as it still contributes a lot to TM$. However, growth has diversified. PSP, Omni, Venmo, and BNPL contributed around half to TM$ growth in FY25. If the growth dynamics continue, the share of the growing and profitable segments increases. And with that, the dependence on the branded checkout button decreases.

CEO Transitioning

The biggest surprise for me was the replacement of Alex Chriss as CEO. I didn’t consider buying a single share of PayPal when Daniel Schulman was CEO. Although he started restructuring by improving the cost structure, I saw the more fundamental problem in the product itself. The PayPal app didn’t see real innovation and improvement for years. Moreover, capital allocation with expensive share buybacks and acquisitions was also a reason not to invest.

Alex Chriss seemed to be the right man for the job. He appeared to be a tech guy who will focus on the product itself and try to unlock value by connecting the different assets PayPal owns. Although he didn’t “shock the world,” I believe that he positioned PayPal better than it was before. Growth was diversified, with Omni, Venmo, and PSP now contributing to half of TM$ growth. Moreover, under his leadership, the number of shares outstanding was reduced from 1.16 billion to around 960 million, and no sizeable and expensive acquisitions were made.

I agree with the board that more was expected on the execution side; however, I also acknowledge the difficulty of turning this ship around.

In March, Enrique Lores will become the new CEO of PayPal. I have mixed feelings here. On the positive side, he's an experienced executive and served on the PayPal board for five years. He was CEO of HP Inc. (HPQ) for six years and helped transition from traditional hardware such as PCs and printers to services, subscriptions, and more. Moreover, he could be a more aggressive cost cutter. SG&A expenses for the company are relatively high, and by improving the cost structure, margins could be strengthened. Additionally, according to CFO Miller, the new approach will be for Branded Checkout to focus on the high-impact large merchants first to implement innovations. I think this approach makes sense, and perhaps Lores will have more success in executing the strategy.

On the other hand, HP Inc.'s stock price development has been disappointing in the last six years as well as the business performance. HP Inc. saw declining profits and stagnating revenue in the last six years. Moreover, he has served on the board of directors for years and can also be seen as responsible for underinvesting under Daniel Schulman’s leadership.

In my view, the replacement of Alex Chriss happened too early. Nevertheless, it is what it is, and I now want to see what Enrique Lores could contribute to the company. However, it will probably take a couple of weeks until investors get more information. This transition in leadership increases uncertainty, as it remains to be seen if the strategy will change and what direction PayPal will take in the coming years.

Valuation

In my view, PayPal could be a compelling buy at current valuations.

First, I will apply a DCF model.

To project future cash flows, I use the FCF margin, which I multiply by (growing) revenue. The FCF margin is between 15% and 20%. In my previous article, I used an 18% margin as a base case and increased it to 19% to reflect a successful transformation into a more profitable company. However, with leadership change and execution being slower in branded checkout, I lower my estimate to 17% in the base case and 18% in the bull case.

I applied an average growth rate of 8% in my last article, which was too optimistic given the current challenges and 4% revenue growth in 2025. I assume 3% now to reflect the challenges in Branded Checkout.

My discount rate of 9% reflects my opportunity cost. I reflect risk not by adjusting the discount rate but by applying a margin of safety. The more uncertain I am about a business, the higher the margin of safety I require. In the case of PayPal, I preferred a margin of safety of at least 20% because I believed it's a well-managed business. However, with leadership transitioning, I prefer a margin of safety of at least 30% now.

To estimate the terminal value, I use the long-term GDP growth rate plus inflation, which I assume to be 2.5%.

In my mid-case scenario, PayPal's fair value is around $75 per share, which offers an upside of more than 90%. In my worst-case scenario, the fair value of the stock is $66. In the best-case scenario, the fair value per share is $80. The stock appears to be undervalued and meets my required margin of safety of 30% in all three scenarios.

The market doesn’t seem to assume revenue growth in the future or that PayPal can maintain those healthy FCF margins. I plugged in different growth rates to get the $40 stock price as my fair value in the base case. I found that the revenue needs to decrease by approximately 11.5% annually for the next five years to get to a fair value per share of $40. Revenue would be around half what it is today. It appears to me that the market is pricing PayPal as a declining, even “dying,” business. Currently, growth has slowed down, but we're far away from double-digit declines. Even if PayPal would not grow at all, the fair value per share would be $66.

Another approach to valuing PayPal is the multiple analysis. PayPal was trading at a P/E of more than 30 for years, as investors considered the company to be the leader in the fast-growing digital payments space. During the pandemic, the P/E ratio even more than doubled to 80. In FY25, PayPal delivered $5.31 in Non-GAAP EPS. At a current stock price of around $40, the P/E ratio is below 8. According to Seeking Alpha, the forward P/E is just 7.3. I believe that a P/E below 8 is too low for a company that is still growing and highly profitable and cash-generative.

Investors could benefit from a multiple expansion. If PayPal proves that it can continue to grow its revenue and earnings, the market has no real reason to price PayPal like an industrial or utility company. The historical average P/E ratio of the S&P 500 is around 17. A multiple above 17 indicates that a business is seen as superior compared to the average American company in the index. A multiple below 17 shows the opposite. In my view, PayPal should trade at a multiple of 13-15 times earnings, as competition is a real risk and Branded Checkout is under pressure. In my view, this makes the company worse than average; however, a P/E of below 8 is too conservative. At a P/E of 13-15, the stock would trade in a range between $69 and $80. This is in alignment with my conservative DCF valuation.

To conclude the valuation of PayPal, I also want to discuss FCF and share repurchases. PayPal has consistently delivered FCF between $5 and $6 billion over the last couple of years. At a market cap of $38 billion, the P/FCF is around 6.4-7.6. If we exclude the share-based compensation of around $1 billion, the P/FCF is between 7.6 and 9.5.

A company can use FCF to reinvest in the business, to reduce debt, or to repurchase shares. Since my previous article, PayPal has repurchased another 30 million shares, and the number of shares outstanding has been reduced by roughly 20% since 2022. I believe that it makes sense to repurchase shares that aggressively, as the stock trades at very low valuations. In the FY26 guidance, repurchases of $6 billion are already planned, which would reduce the share count by another 13% excluding the share-based compensation.

Risks

First, PayPal could lose market share to competitors as the competitive landscape becomes more crowded. PayPal competes with Apple Pay, Google Pay, and many others in the Branded Checkout. It faces Adyen and Stripe as competitors in the unbranded checkout. Venmo competes with Block's Cash App and Zelle. Moreover, Trade Republic and Revolut offer debit cards for offline payments. Additionally, PayPal's BNPL competes directly with Klarna.

In my previous article, I mentioned the key man risk of PayPal.

Moreover, Alex Chriss is crucial for executing this strategy, and my valuation depends on him remaining as CEO. He has laid out the roadmap for PayPal and has demonstrated his ability to improve operational efficiency and enhance its product offering. If he were to leave or fail to execute effectively, this could have a negative effect on growth, and I would need to adjust my growth assumptions.

Exactly that happened, and I adjusted my growth assumptions accordingly. I believe that execution remains a key risk, as PayPal is in the middle of an important transition phase.

Summary

PayPal has not been standing still in the last two years. Growth has been diversified; PSP, Venmo, Omni, and BNPL are growing double digits and taking a larger share of PayPal’s total TPV. However, the challenges Branded Checkout faces are worse than I first assumed. Moreover, with Alex Chriss leaving the company, uncertainty increased, and I adjusted my growth assumptions. At a current market cap of $38 billion, PayPal appears to be deeply undervalued and priced for failure. Howard Marks put it well: "There is no such thing as a bargain-priced asset that everybody loves." I believe that PayPal could be an attractive opportunity. But keep in mind to reduce position size, as uncertainty remains.

data.title: Gold And Precious Metals Slide: Iran Diplomacy And Lunar New Year Drain Liquidity
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Happy Lunar New Year! Many Asian nations and people celebrate the beginning of the year of the Fire Horse, taking many essential commodity markets off for this week.

As detailed in our past day's oil outlook, China being off can amplify moves in commodity markets, as the Shanghai Futures Exchange (where most Asian trading for metals occurs) is closed for the entire week, leading to thinner markets.

After a long US weekend, traders are unloading their risk hedges amid mostly positive communications regarding the second round of US-Iran talks in Geneva.

Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi communicated a potential exchange of documents, which follows the more recent narrative of higher odds of a deal, expressed by Israeli PM Netanyahu on his way back from the White House.

One of the key reasons for metals to rally exponentially at the beginning of 2026 was the sudden pricing in of a potential significant conflict in the Middle East after brutal repressions and tragic death tolls from the revolts.

Combined with the conclusion of a very positive seasonal month for metals, February came with a cold wind for precious commodity bulls.

While gold (XAUUSD:CUR) remains on top, silver (XAGUSD:CUR) and other, more volatile and less safe-haven metals are getting rejected quite starkly. Note that platinum (XPTUSD:CUR) seems quite resilient above $2,000, so keep an eye on this one.

With positioning getting to extremes (as expressed in a recent Bank of America Survey), options amassing for $15,000-20,000 prices in gold (typical of market tops), and fundamentals not warranting a continuous, clear-cut path to doubling prices, metals could be facing a fragile outlook ahead.

Of course, with a second US warship heading to the Middle East, which should arrive in about a week, it will be challenging to say. Geopolitics are all about deception, particularly in the age of information.

But in the meantime, with US rates not expected to change until at least May, dollar positioning at its lowest, and metals overcrowded, the narrative seems to be turning for markets, may it be for AI or metals.

After a year-long straightforward debasement trade, things could suddenly not be so easy.

Let's dive right into an in-depth look at gold as traders prepare for a heavy slate of macroeconomic data throughout the week.

Gold Multi-Time Frame Technical Analysis

Daily Chart

XAU is rejecting not only its essential $5,100 resistance but also is accompanied by a descending trendline, having broken its triangle formation to the downside.

The Daily RSI is also moving in that direction, looking poised to cross the bearish mid-level soon.

Taking a step back, gold is simply rejecting its upside breakout and reverting to a more stable, continuous bull channel it has followed since 2024, which can provide more sustainable entry levels.

Dip-buyers will want to pay close attention to the 50-day MA ($4,657), which acted as key support throughout the entire bull trend. A rebound there should see continuation. But watch out if prices actually close below on any session.

4H Chart and Technical Levels

Still, the pivot zone is coming in as a huge test for what's coming, particularly as the 4H 200-MA acts as its final support ($4,812). Any 4H close below should lead to at least a retest of the February support at $4,400.

Levels to Watch for Gold (XAU/USD):

  • $5,100 key resistance
  • 4H 50 MA $5,000
  • Current all-time highs - $5,500-5,600
  • $5,400 mini-resistance

Support Levels

  • 4H 200-MA $4,812
  • Pivotal support $4,400-4,500
  • $4,100 main channel support lows
  • Main support $3,880 to $4,050
  • $3,200-3,500 major support

1H Chart

The session lows will be acting as a key indicator for the coming session. Closing below will first test the 200 4H-MA, while breaking it points to further downside.

data.title: First Eagle Short Duration High Yield Municipal Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

During the fourth quarter, the municipal bond market continued to rebound from the challenges of the first half of 2025.

Record-setting bond issuance continued to be met by strong demand, enabling muni indexes to post positive returns for the quarter and the year. The S&P Municipal Bond High Yield Index gained 1.5% during the fourth quarter, while the S&P Municipal Yield Index, which includes bonds across the quality spectrum, rose 1.7% and the S&P Short Duration Municipal Yield Index advanced 0.6%. Those indexes were up a respective 3.3%, 3.5% and 4.3% in full-year 2025. For context, the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index gained 1.1% during the quarter and 7.3% in the year.1

Market Able to Absorb Record Levels of Muni Issuance

Though the pace of muni bond issuance eased somewhat in the fourth quarter, the $100 billion-plus of new issuance during the period was enough to drive the market to a new annual high of $580 billion, about 13% above the previous record set in 2024. It’s worth noting that the vast majority of issuance during the year was new capital, with refunding activity accounting for only about 12%.2 Lower levels of refunding transactions suggest fewer bonds are being called, which can help support valuations for the market as a whole.

We were not surprised to see the municipal bond curve steepen considerably during 2025. The flood of issuance—high yield muni issuance in particular, given its long-dated nature, generally provides a steepening impulse to the yield curve—pushed long yields higher, although Federal Reserve policy rate cuts weighed on the short end of the muni curve.3 The US Treasury curve also steepened over the year as term premia returned to the Treasury market after being mostly negative for nearly a decade.4

There are a few factors we believe have contributed to the ongoing surge in issuance. After sitting on the sidelines during the 2022–23 rate-hike period, municipalities have a pent-up need to issue paper as the benefits of Covid-era federal funding and post-pandemic tax receipts wane. Meanwhile, pretty much every capital project costs more today from the impact of inflation across inputs like steel, concrete and lumber, and increased costs for civil engineering, skilled and unskilled labor, etc. Further, potential legislative threats to muni bond tax treatment in the first half of the year—since alleviated—likely pulled forward some issuance.

Fortunately, the US government shutdown that began on October 1 did not have the same chilling effect on demand as the Liberation Day tariff announcements in April, and massive issuance was met by robust demand throughout the fourth quarter. Flows into municipal bond mutual funds and exchange-traded funds ended the year strong, amounting to about $23 billion in the fourth quarter, which represented more than one-third of full-year 2025 flows.5

The Fed’s dovish tilt in the second half of the year has also supported demand for muni bonds. Though it was cut off from official federal agency data during the government shutdown, the Fed followed up its September rate cut with another in October. The catch-up in economic data flow, after federal employees returned to work in mid-November, did little to change the central bank’s viewpoint, which seemed to center more on a weakening labor market than inflation concerns, and an additional December cut brought the federal funds rate down to 3.50–3.75% to end the year.6

The Fed appears well-positioned to wait and see how the economy evolves, and the dot plot of rate projections published in December points to only one additional 25 basis point cut in 2026.7 That said, the Trump administration’s very public desire for lower rates and conflict with Fed Chair Powell—highlighted by early-January reports that the Department of Justice had opened a criminal investigation into Powell related to the renovation of the Fed’s headquarters—suggest that whoever replaces Powell when his term as Fed chair ends in May 2026 is likely to be more dovish than the average governor. In a world of increasingly lower floating-rate yields, investors may increasingly view fixed-rate municipal bonds as a more appealing cash equivalent.

Fundamentals Remain Steady

The initial estimate of third quarter GDP for the US came in at 4.3%, up from 3.8% in the second quarter and well above the consensus estimate of 3.3%. While growth in the fourth quarter is likely to be negatively impacted by the government shutdown, fiscal stimulus from the 2025 tax bill and potential monetary stimulus should be supportive of economic activity in 2026, to the ongoing benefit of municipal issuer fundamentals.8

Municipalities entered 2025 in strong fiscal condition, and issuer fundamentals continue to be supportive. State budgets for fiscal 2026 overall reflect a healthy environment, and general fund balances remain well above the historical average even as they continue to ease from 2023’s peak. Though state general fund revenue has fallen off the record pace of fiscal 2021 and 2022 as the impact of Covid–era relief waned, it has continued to grow, and modest revenue gains are expected in fiscal 2026. Budgets enacted to date suggest flat general fund spending in 2026, and most states plan to maintain or increase the size of their rainy-day fund—many of which are already at nominal highs—in anticipation of future needs.9

Another sign of fiscal strength can be found in improved pension funding, as the aggregate median ratio for local-government pensions climbed to 80% in fiscal 2024 from 78% in fiscal 2022. While this can be attributed in part to market performance, local governments have increased contributions and tweaked their benefit structures, demonstrating improved funding discipline and better long-term sustainability.10

Overall, muni bond ratings activity has been positive in 2025, but not by much: Positive activity (including both upgrades and favorable outlook revisions) outpaced negative activity at a rate of 1.4x year to date through November.11 Both defaults and first-time distressed debt remained very low in 2025.12

Start of a Turnaround?

Muni bond performance in 2025 was a tale of two halves, and we’re encouraged by the asset class’s momentum in the second half. It seems likely to us that the factors driving recent performance and fund flows—credit stability, certainty around tax treatment, an accommodative Fed and relatively benign tariff impacts—will continue to support the asset class, even in what is expected to be another year of robust issuance.

As a team, we remain focused on seeking to provide a high level of tax-free current income through bottom-up security selection complemented by an active trading strategy and the prudent application of leverage. This approach, combined with consistent fund inflows, has enabled us to increase the interest income generated by the portfolio in 2025, and we have passed this along to shareholders in the form of a higher monthly distribution rate.13

1 Source: FactSet; data as of December 31, 2025.

2 Source: Municipal Securities Rulemaking Board; data as of December 31, 2025.

3 Source: Municipal Securities Rulemaking Board; data as of December, 2025.

4 Source: Federal Reserve Bank of New York; data as of December 31, 2025.

5 Source: Investment Company Institute; data as of December 31, 2025.

8 Source: Reuters; data as of December 23, 2025.

10 Source: S&P Global; data as of September 16, 2025.

11 Source: S&P Global; data as of September 30, 2025.

12 Source: BofA Global Research; data as of January 9, 2026.

Portfolio Review

Short Duration High Yield Municipal Fund A Shares (FDUAX)(without sales charge*) posted a return of –0.09% in fourth quarter 2025. The Fund underperformed the S&P Short Duration Municipal Index in the period.

One area of the municipal bond market we believe is particularly rich with opportunity is the healthcare sector, and the Fund maintains a significant allocation to it. Though the sector had begun to recover from the dislocations of Covid-19 in 2023–24, investors in 2025 grew concerned about the potential impacts of Trump’s tax-and-spending bill, which included lower reimbursement rates, lower utilization rates and pressure on federal and state aid.

We think these concerns may be premature. Cuts to Medicaid and Medicare—which comprise approximately 44% of US hospital spending1— outlined in the bill will total more than $1 trillion through 2034.2 Though set to begin in 2026, many of these cuts will ramp up over time, which we believe will give hospitals, healthcare providers and insurance carriers time to adjust their operating models. It may also allow time for opponents of the cuts opportunities to push back on or eliminate their implementation.

Within healthcare, we believe that larger, well-managed hospital systems, specialty-care hospitals and hospitals that provide essential care in geographies with population growth and a favorable payer mix are more likely to be resilient in the face of policy changes. Bonds linked to the expansion of two such hospital systems were among the top contributors to fourth-quarter performance: Westchester Medical Center serving the New York tristate area, and Loma Linda University Health in Southern California.

Bonds related to the Brightline passenger rail project in Florida comprised the biggest detractors to performance in the fourth quarter. Brightline, which is backed by private equity firm Fortress Investment Group, is the only privately owned and operated intercity railroad in the US. It began service in Florida in 2018 and has steadily increased its footprint along the east coast of the state from Miami to Orlando and has plans to expand its network from Orlando to Tampa.

These bonds came under fire in 2025 and were subject to downgrades from rating agencies citing lower-than-expected ridership and higher costs as well as dwindling cash reserves and other liquidity concerns. In July, Brightline announced that it would defer its mid-month interest payment on certain tranches of debt, news to which markets reacted very negatively, which we believe was an overreaction. Outstanding Brightline bonds continue to trade at deep discounts.

1Source: KFF; data as of September 3, 2025.

2Source: Congressional Budget Office; data as of July 4, 2025.

* Performance for Class A shares without the effect of sales charges and assumes all distributions have been reinvested, and if a sales charge was included values would be lower.

Despite Brightline’s recent difficulties, we remain constructive on both the project and the bonds. The negative market sentiment largely has been priced into the bonds, in our view, substantially reducing our downside risk in 2026 while enhancing our upside potential. Meanwhile, recent trends at the company have been bullish.

Operational improvements in 2025—including the addition of 30 new train cars and a complete schedule revamp to improve network optimization—have put Brightline on the verge of finally generating positive monthly cash flow. This operational momentum will have a direct impact on the ability of Fortress Investments (Brightline’s owner) to attract equity investors and raise substantial additional funds to improve Brightline’s capital structure and pay down higher coupon debt as well as deferred interest. We have been told that potential equity investors are spending significant time and money on due diligence efforts.

In January, Brightline announced the appointment of Nicolas Petrovic as CEO of Brightline Holdings LLC. Petrovic has more than 25 years of rail experience, with leadership positions across Europe and the Middle East, including with Eurostar, Siemens France and, most recently, Etihad Rail Mobility. In our view, Brightline’s ability to lure such a tenured and respected CEO to Florida at this stage of his career speaks volumes about the company’s prospects going forward.

In short, we believe outstanding Brightline bonds have been oversold and that the combination of operational momentum, new leadership and a likely equity infusion suggests substantial recovery potential.

We appreciate your confidence and thank you for your support.

Sincerely,

data.title: Stock Market's Hot Streak Is Expected To Cool In The Decade Ahead
data.description:
By James Picerno

The S&P 500 Index’s (SPX) trailing ten-year return has edged up since our previous performance outlook in November, but today’s revised forecast for the decade ahead is still holding in the mid-5% annualized range.

That’s a modest estimate of future performance relative to the double-digit returns in recent years. The current ten-year gain clocks in at a 13.5% annualized total return, more than a percentage point higher compared to when we ran the numbers in late November.

Although the market has turned relatively sedate so far in 2026 (the S&P is roughly flat year to date through Feb. 13), the rear-view mirror continues to highlight a roaring bull market. Consider: the trailing 13.5% ten-year gain is nearly double the market’s 7.4% average annualized return since 1960.

Will the market’s turbocharged performance continue? The bulls offer several scenarios for expecting the party to endure, including an AI-fueled rise in corporate profits and productivity, a resilient US economy, and expectations that the Federal Reserve will provide more liquidity support through ongoing rate cuts this year.

The question is whether the market’s fully valued and already pricing in those and other best-case-scenario assumptions. In other words, is this as good as it gets?

The future’s always uncertain, but it’s useful to run the numbers on several models to review changes in expectations versus how the current outlook compares with history. TMC Research updated the five models that we routinely monitor, and the results indicate that the S&P 500’s expected return for the decade ahead still looks relatively modest next to the sizzling run in recent history.

The average performance forecast for the ten years through 2036 is 5.6% annualized. That’s a tick above November’s forecast and (still) far below the market’s 13.5% trailing return over the past decade.

Forecasts should always be viewed cautiously, but the large gap between ex ante and ex post performance suggests some degree of mean reversion may be brewing. All of which boils down to the age-old question: Is it different this time? If so, why?

The future isn’t a slave to the past, but with a market outperforming so dramatically, the burden of proof is increasingly on the bulls to justify why lofty return expectations are still a reasonable forecast.

Here’s a quick review of each of the five models used to generate the forecasts above:

CAPE Ratio Model: This stock market valuation indicator, maintained by Professor Robert Shiller, is calculated using real earnings per share for the S&P 500 based on a rolling 10-year window. TMC Research uses the CAPE ratio to generate an implied return for the stock market.

Earnings Yield Model: The stock market’s implied ex ante performance is derived from the S&P 500’s earning yield, defined as the inverse of the price-to-earnings ratio.

ARIMA Model: In contrast with the two models above, which use valuation to infer future return, this estimate uses statistical analysis to generate a forecast. An autoregressive integrated moving average (ARIMA) model is a type of regression analysis that’s run on rolling window of the market’s return history to estimate future results.

Bayesian Model: This statistical model uses lags as a basis for updating so-called “prior beliefs” on a rolling basis to estimate the effects of previous effects on the S&P 500 to forecast return.

Average Historical Return Model: This naïve estimate is a simple average of all the rolling 10-year returns since 1960.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: Fanuc Roaring Back On Improving Industrial Sentiment
data.description:
Industrial markets are coming back to life, and with that, so too is demand for key factory automation inputs like machine tools and robotics. That’s good news for Fanuc (FANUY) (FANUF) (6954.T), and this automation leader has seen its share price more than double from its April 2025 lows and rise more than 50% in the last six months.

It’s been a while since I’ve written on Fanuc, and over that time the company has seen its business mix shift even more towards robotics, and I believe Fanuc has out-executed its peers in driving robotics into newer markets like aerospace and industrial precision assembly. Moreover, with strong internal capabilities in servomotors, vision, control, and software, I believe Fanuc is well-placed for a future where industrial robots are expected to be more independent, more flexible, and more capable.

There aren’t a lot of obviously cheap automation names left in the market, and Fanuc has typically only traded at attractive valuations in cyclical troughs. I do see a real possibility of beat-and-raise quarters over the next couple of years, and the valuation isn’t unreasonable now. This is not a classic value call by any means, but given improving end markets and strong strategic positioning, I can still see a case for owning these shares today.

Demand is Recovering, but So Are Expectations

Fanuc had a decent recent run of better-than-expected quarterly results, but between volatile forex and rising expectations, the recent fiscal third quarter was less impressive relative to sell-side expectations. There was nevertheless ongoing evidence of market and business recovery, and I believe that is the more important takeaway.

Revenue rose almost 10% year over year and about 2% quarter over quarter in the fiscal third quarter, which was basically as expected. Factory Automation revenue (23% of total) rose 4% YoY and fell 8% QoQ, Robot revenue (45% of total) rose 22% YoY and 6% QoQ, and Robomachine revenue (15% of total) fell 8% YoY and rose 12% QoQ. Service revenue (17% of total) rose 7% YoY and 1% QoQ.

Gross margin improved 170 bp YoY and fell 100 bp to 36.4%. Operating income rose 20% YoY and fell 4% QoQ, with margin up 160 bp YoY and down 130 bp QoQ to 19.3%. Operating income missed by 8%, the first miss in a while, but it appears that this was driven by forex moves and nothing operational.

Compared to peers, Fanuc outgrew Yaskawa in its robotics business (up 22% versus up 9%) and likewise outperformed ABB (ABBNY) (where robotics revenue was down 2% YoY). With ABB selling the robots business to SoftBank (SFTBY), management didn’t really discuss it, and it’s unclear to what extent the separation may be impacting near-term results.

It’s harder to benchmark the Factory Automation segment given a lack of clean overlap; Siemens (SIEGY) competes in CNC/motion control systems, but has many other businesses included in Digital Industries, and likewise for Yaskawa and Mitsubishi Electric (MIELY). In any case, Siemens reported 13% growth in DI, Yaskawa reported a 2% decline in Motion Control, and Mitsubishi Electric reported a 10% sales improvement in its Factory Automation sub-segment.

Business is Ramping Back Up, Including Auto Demand

There are multiple data points that suggest industrial demand is recovering, including the first 50+ reading for U.S. PMI in quite some time and double-digit growth in robotics and machine tools in Japan in the fourth quarter. Likewise, many industrial conglomerates have reported on-target or better sales through this reporting cycle, with most guiding to improving demand. Last but not least, a low inventory reading within that U.S. PMI report suggests scope for production growth (and tooling/equipment demand to support it) in the quarters ahead.

Fanuc reported 9% overall order growth for the third quarter, with Factory Automation orders up 16% and Robot orders up 21%. FA book-to-bill improved to 1.1x, while Robot was 1.02x.

Fanuc management sounds particularly bullish on the U.S. market and has for some time now. Interestingly, management cited improving auto and auto part demand as a driver for the robotics business, including “solid” demand related to EV production/launches; curious to me given the generally negative tenor around EV demand and production volumes in the United States.

Longer-Term Opportunities in New Markets

I do expect a very good run for Fanuc in the United States in this new upcycle. There is no meaningful local competition for Fanuc in the U.S. (Teradyne’s (TER) cobots are not generally used in assembly/manufacturing), and the company enjoys top share in industrial categories. This positions Fanuc to not only benefit from increasing reshoring and ongoing shortages of skilled labor but also growing robotics penetration into new markets.

While auto OEMs and suppliers have long been major users of robots, industries like aerospace and precision assembly are starting to adopt them more significantly. In aerospace, Fanuc has been winning business for precision drilling/riveting applications, as well as precision machining and other tasks like deburring, polishing, and material handling. This also isn’t just a U.S. phenomenon, as Fanuc has won meaningful business with Airbus (EADSY).

Fanuc has also been investing significant resources in areas like vision, fine motor control, and feedback, and recently demoed a robotic station capable of making sandwiches (which were reportedly then served to attendees). I’m not suggesting that delis are going to start installing robotic systems, but I think it underlines the investments that Fanuc has been making to increase the capabilities of its systems and expand the addressable markets outside of traditional welding, large-scale assembly, and pick-and-place tasks.

Fanuc has also been investing in AI capabilities and noted over 1,000 unit orders for its new Physical AI-enabled CRX robots. This is relatively limited AI at this point, but it does suggest to me that Fanuc is maintaining its “fast follower” R&D strategy, and it stands in contrast to Yaskawa, ABB, and KUKA that have been slower to adapt to new trends and technologies and further behind in some of these more advanced capabilities.

What Does the Future Hold for Robomachines?

I expect big things from Fanuc’s robotics business in the coming years. I’m not quite as bullish on the Factory Automation segment given rising competition, but the company still has very strong overall global share (around 50%), and competitors have found it tougher to make headway against Fanuc in higher-end segments.

Robomachines is facing a tougher future. Demand for products like tapping centers (Robodrills), injection molding equipment, and so on has declined with fewer significant form factor changes between generations of smartphones and other electronics, and increased competition from new Chinese, Korean, and Taiwanese entrants has also impacted other parts of the business (like auto-related demand).

I see some opportunities to benefit from ongoing manufacturing relocation, not only back into developed markets like the U.S. and EU, but also from China to countries like Vietnam. Those relocations drive new tooling demand, and there will still be some generational changes in smartphones et al. that will drive retooling demand.

Even so, I don’t see this business growing back to 33% plus of the sales mix, and that will be an ongoing headwind on margin expansion, as this was a highly profitable business at scale.

The Outlook

I’m expecting double-digit revenue growth in FY'27 as Fanuc leverages recovery demand in machine tools and further growth in robotics to support capacity expansions and manufacturing reshoring. I don’t expect a surge in auto capex, but there should be some recovery from weak levels, and I do expect healthy demand in areas like semiconductors, aerospace, and industrial material handling.

I’m looking for around 8% growth over the next three years, with longer-term growth settling down to around 6% on an annualized basis.

Furthermore, I expect improved operational leverage and growth in new, more capable, higher-ASP systems to drive EBITDA margin from the high 20%s in recent years to almost 32% in FY'27 and then further improvement toward the mid 30%s. This, in turn, should drive free cash flow margins in the high teens and normalized FCF growth in the high single digits over the longer term.

One caveat here is what the future pace of capex will look like—historically, Fanuc has spent over 10% of revenue on capex annually, but that has dropped to around 5% in recent years. Fanuc has surplus capacity at present and likely won’t need to add capacity at the same pace as earlier in the past decade, but I’m always a little nervous about modeling substantial shifts like this.

To some extent the capex question is moot – Fanuc doesn’t look undervalued on discounted cash flow and almost never has, apart from those times when the market is really bearish on factory automation. Likewise, there has historically been a strong relationship between Fanuc’s ROE and its book value multiple, and it’s already trading at a premium that anticipates the recovery.

An EV/EBITDA approach is more forgiving. Fanuc has strong margins and ROIC, as well as above-average near-term growth potential, and this can support a high-teens multiple today. A forward multiple of 19.25x gives me a fair value about 5% ahead of today’s price. I’m not a fan of P/E-based valuation, but I will note that Fanuc is roughly in the middle of its historical range, and a 32.5x multiple, in line with prior early up-cycles on my FY'27 EPS, would support around 5% upside from here.

The Bottom Line

Fanuc has seen big trough-to-peak runs in the past (both for the financials and the share price), and I may well be underestimating how strong this recovery cycle will be. Coupled with a little undervaluation and what I believe is a strong competitive position in the evolving leading edge of robotics, I lean more favorably here.

Valuation is a concern, and this pending industrial recovery could still disappoint, but I like the prospects for beat-and-raise quarters. This isn’t a value call, but I do think there’s growth, momentum, and strategic position in place that can allow this name to work from here.

data.title: GSK: Post-Pivot Buoyancy Set To Continue After Strong 2025 (Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Overview

The last time I wrote about GSK plc (GSK) for Seeking Alpha, it was late September, and I was covering the news that its CEO since 2017, Emma Walmsley, was stepping away from the role to be replaced by the current Chief Commercial Officer ("CCO") Luke Miels.

I suggested that Walmsley's tenure may be best remembered for the spinout of GSK's consumer health division into a new entity, Haleon (HLN), and for battles with activist investors who believed the company (and its share price) were underperforming.

In a letter from 2021, responding to the activist investor Eliott Advisors, GSK noted:

  • Over the period 2021-26, New GSK expects to deliver sales growth and adjusted operating profit growth of more than 5% and 10% respectively CAGR at constant exchange rates (with 2021 as the base year). Adjusted operating margins are expected to improve from the current mid-20s level to over 30% by 2026.
  • By 2031, New GSK aims to deliver sales of more than £33 billion (at constant exchange rates). The £33 billion sales ambition is before any significant revenue contribution from early-stage pipeline assets or any contribution from business development.
  • Cash generated from operations is expected to exceed £10 billion by 2026. New GSK is expected to have a stronger balance sheet (net debt / adjusted EBITDA of <2x following separation) and a progressive dividend policy starting at 45p per share in 2023.

On 4th February this year, GSK announced its Q4 and full-year results. The pharma reported £32.7bn of revenues, meaning it has very nearly reached its 2031 revenue target already, and by my calculation, the CAGR growth rate between 2021 and 2025 is ~7.5%.

Cash generated by operations in 2025 was £8.9bn, close to the £10bn target with one year to go, and core operating profit in 2025 was reported as £9.8bn, up 11% year-on-year. Core operating margin came to 29.9%.

In summary, broadly speaking, we can say that GSK is delivering on its pledges, and not only that, GSK stock is up >65% on a five-year basis, with most of those gains having been realized within the last 6 months.

We may speculate then that Wall Street believes in GSK, its products, and its potential - more so than I believed it would when I assigned the stock a Hold rating in September last year.

In this update, I'll focus on Q4 and full-year 2025 results and look ahead to the remainder of 2026 and beyond, highlighting strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats.

Analysis GSK - 2025 Performance Highlights & Looking Further Ahead

In 2025, by my calculation, GSK's HIV division, consisting of seven major drugs, earned revenues of ~£7.7bn, up ~8% year-on-year, and accounted for 24% of total revenues.

The Oncology division, with four major products, earned $1.98bn, or 6% of total revenues; Respiratory, with two major products, £3.8bn, 15% of revenues; and its vaccines division, ~13 major products, $9.2bn, or ~28% of total revenues. Other General Medicines, which include multiple other asthma/COPD therapies and a range of older, patent expired drugs, contributed another ~£5.93bn.

As we can see in the graphic above (taken from GSK's earnings presentation), Specialty medicines - i.e., HIV, respiratory, and oncology products that remain patent protected—are the Pharma's fastest-growing division and the biggest contributor to revenues. The U.S. is also GSK's largest market.

The growth in specialty medicines will doubtless please Wall Street because these patent-protected drugs can be sold at a high price, with no generic competition, and therefore drive the highest profit margins.

In that sense, the 40% growth of the oncology division, 15% growth of Respiratory, and 8% HIV growth are impressive, and on its earnings call with analysts, management forecast "low double-digit" percentage growth for Specialty Medicines in 2026. Overall, 2026 guidance is for sales growth of 3-5% and core operating profit and earnings per share ("EPS") growth of 7-9%.

Looking slightly further ahead, I would have some concerns around the future patent expirations of certain of GSK's drugs. Based on information shared in the company's 2024 annual report/20-F filing, numerous HIV, respiratory, and oncology drugs look set to lose patent protections either just before or just after the end of the decade:

While these "patent cliffs" remain some years away, my research suggests that drug products that account for close to ~50% of GSK's revenues in 2025 are threatened by a patent expiry within the next five years.

Attention, therefore, turns to GSK's pipeline - does the company have the ability to secure commercial approval for and successfully launch new drugs to offset falling revenues of patent expired products in the next decade?

In 2025, GSK reports it secured FDA approvals for five products - meningococcal vaccine Penmenvy, Blujepa for uncomplicated urinary tract infections, a label expansion for Nucala to treat Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease ("COPD"), and for Blenrep, to treat relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma ("R/R MM"), and finally for Asthma therapy Exdensur.

These assets could contribute >$5bn to GSK's top line by 2030, based on analysts' estimates, and we may also note the Pharma initiated seven new Phase 3 clinical study starts in 2025, including Exdensur in COPD, efimosfermin in metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (“MASH”), velzatinib in second line gastrointestinal stromal tumors (“GIST”), and a small cell lung cancer therapy, "ris-rez."

As we can see above, 2026 - 2027 could see multiple new therapies launched, not only those I have discussed already but also the RNA-interference therapy bepirovirsen in chronic hepatitis B, kidney cancer med camlipixant, multiple new combinations of existing HIV therapies including long-acting cabotegravir (Cabenuva), and others besides.

Analysis - Concluding Thoughts - Steady Growth, Strong Pipeline Supports Bull Case

GSK's current share price is $59, and market cap valuation is $119bn, which is somewhat low for a company that earned ~$44bn of revenues last year, with operating income of $12.7bn - ratios of ~2.7x and ~9x, respectively, when the industry average is closer to ~5x and 15–20x, my research suggests.

This supports the case that GSK remains an undervalued company and that its successful pivot into becoming a "pure-play" pharmaceutical drug developer may well be rewarded with further share price upside in 2026.

As mentioned, a potential cloud on the horizon is looming patent expiries for many drugs; however, my research suggests losses can be offset by new drug launches, and I believe GSK may be able to grow its top line at a CAGR of ~6% to 2030 and at a rate in the low single digits in the early years of the next decade.

Another concern is around headwinds facing its vaccine division, GSK's second largest division by revenues, with the current healthcare administration in the U.S. considered to be vaccine sceptic.

Furthermore, the Trump administration has threatened to impose tariffs on pharma imports into the U.S. in a bid to encourage more Pharma to do their manufacturing in the U.S. and threatened to impose a "most favored nation" drug pricing policy in the U.S., forcing drug companies to slash prices in the region.

In December last year, however, GSK struck a deal with the U.S. government around drug pricing, amongst other things agreeing to "make most of its inhaled respiratory portfolio and other products available to patients on a direct purchasing platform that will offer savings of up to 66%," and to "invest more than $30bn in R&D and manufacturing in the U.S. over the next 5 years."

While these risks must be acknowledged, it seems to me they may be already factored into GSK's low valuation and also that they are not serious enough risks to prevent GSK from being able to grow revenues and profits over time, with its pipeline focus shifting away from vaccines and into respiratory and oncology, for example.

TradingView reports a dividend yield for GSK shareholders of nearly 3%, and the company reported net debt to EBITDA of ~1.3x as of the end of Q4 2025, and long-term debt of ~$19.83bn, which is comparatively low for the "Big Pharma" industry, and may mean the company can continue to engage in M&A activities - recently, GSK acquired Rapt Therapeutics and its lead asset ozureprubart, a "potentially best-in-class anti-IgE antibody, in development for prophylactic protection against food allergens," in a deal worth up to $2.2bn.

To conclude, then, steady top and bottom line growth, a strong pipeline (to offset patent-expiring drugs), a healthy debt position, and a generous dividend payout all support a Buy rating for GSK, further underscored by the Pharma's low valuation relative to peers.

The jury may have been out over the past few years as GSK has completed its pivot into a pure play Pharma, but in my view, we may call this pivot an overall success, and shareholders may reap the benefits of that over the coming years.

By 2030, I'd set GSK a revenue target of >$55bn, which, applying a conservative forward P/S ratio of 3x, suggests to me a target market cap of ~$165bn, implying share price growth potential of >35% by 2030.

data.title: IAT: Undervalued Regional Banks With Strong Growth Momentum
data.description:
Investment Thesis

iShares U.S. Regional Banks ETF (NYSEARCA:IAT) warrants a buy rating due to the qualities of its top holdings, including growth momentum, profitability, and sustainable dividends. Regional bank funds typically experience greater volatility than broader financial ETFs. However, recent U.S. economic data points towards economic strength that will result in IAT’s outperformance. Furthermore, the fund’s current valuation will drive strong capital appreciation for the remainder of 2026.

iShares U.S. Regional Banks ETF—Overview and Compared ETFs

IAT is an ETF that seeks to capture small and mid-size banks in the United States. With its inception in 2006, the fund has 31 holdings and $635M in AUM. As a banking ETF, IAT is inherently less diversified than broader financial sector funds. This concentration involves greater risk but can also drive stronger returns in certain economic conditions, as I will discuss later.

For comparison purposes, other funds examined are First Trust NASDAQ ABA Community Bank Index Fund (QABA), Invesco KBW Bank ETF (KBWB), and Vanguard Financials ETF (VFH). QABA includes Nasdaq-listed banks but excludes the 50 largest banks and any banks classified with international or credit card specializations. KBWB differs from IAT and QABA in that it uses a modified market-cap weighted strategy to capture the largest banks and money centers. VFH broadly seeks to capture the financial sector and includes banks, insurance companies, and payment processing companies.

IAT Compared: Performance, Expense Ratio, and Dividend Yield

IAT has underperformed the broader market as measured by the S&P 500 Index, with a 10-year average annual return of 7.60%. By comparison, QABA, which also excludes the largest banks, has seen a 10-year average return of 7.74%. KBWB and VFH, which capture the largest banks and the broader financial sector, saw 10-year average annual returns of 12.98% and 13.77%, respectively. For reasons discussed later, I believe IAT will see stronger-than-average returns in the coming years.

IAT has an average expense ratio compared to peer financial funds with a fee of 0.38%. A key advantage of the iShares fund is its dividend yield of 2.72%. This yield is higher than all the funds compared and will likely continue to be relatively high due to the income-producing qualities of the fund’s top holdings covered later.

IAT Holdings and Key Outlook Factors

While other funds have different objectives, IAT’s top holdings are also found in other ETFs. That is, they are smaller than the largest banks, like Bank of America (BAC) or Wells Fargo (WFC), found in VFH’s top holdings.
